acceptance_targets: [CH1_CRAFT_CONTINUITY_VIAGRA_SETUP] Pass only if a reader of the revised chapter can state, from the text itself, that Tommy gives Martin one Viagra pill on Friday night rather than a four-pill bag on Thursday, and no later line in the chapter preserves the old contradictory object state.

[CH1_CRAFT_CONTINUITY_RECORDER_GEOGRAPHY] Pass only if the revised snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:307 lets a cold reader map Nate in the basement and the recorder in the kitchen without any floor-count contradiction; the line must name a spatial relationship that matches the established three-level house.

[CH1_CRAFT_CLIFF_DEBT_SETUP_TOO_COY] Pass only if, by the end of the Burnsville exchange in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:251 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:255, a cold reader can answer "What does Martin owe Cliff?" with something more specific than "some business issue" and can tell that the unresolved money matters now.

[CH1_CRAFT_DISCOVERY_TRIGGER_UNDERSEEDED] Pass only if, before Nate goes upstairs in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:417, the revised chapter has already established on page that he and Martin customarily settle the last-place bet late at night or that Nate has another concrete, previously introduced reason to look for him. When the body-discovery beat arrives, the reader should be recalling a setup rather than learning it for the first time.

[COMEDY_REG_CH01] Pass if chapter 01 contains at least three exchanges of crude/raunchy humor that go beyond clever wit, Tommy's dialogue profanity density matches his 'very high' profile, and the hot tub scene includes shown dialogue rather than summarized conversation. Fail if the comedy register remains at its current level of polish and moderate profanity.
[RED-020] Chapter 1 contains at most 2 loon references.
[CON-008] The real estate deal location is named consistently across Chs 1 and 8.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass only if a reader of the revised chapter can state, from the text itself, that Tommy gives Martin one Viagra pill on Friday night rather than a four-pill bag on Thursday, and no later line in the chapter preserves the old contradictory object state.
findings.problem: The chapter contradicts the canonical setup for the Viagra evidence. On the page Tommy gives Martin a bag containing four pills on Thursday night and Martin pockets the whole bag immediately, but the continuity sheet and chapter-boundary setup establish a single-pill Friday-evening gag beat. That changes the timing, quantity, and object state that later chapters rely on when Tommy panics about the pills in Martin's room.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:191, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:213, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:221 so the on-page action matches the canonical setup: either move the actual handoff to Friday evening and make it a single-pill joke, or keep Thursday as verbal teasing and reserve the physical pill beat for the later night. If that change alters Nate's recollection, also adjust snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:305 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:409 to stay consistent.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass only if the revised snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:307 lets a cold reader map Nate in the basement and the recorder in the kitchen without any floor-count contradiction; the line must name a spatial relationship that matches the established three-level house.
findings.problem: The recorder location is spatially impossible. Nate is in the basement, but the line says the phone and mic are in the kitchen "three floors up." The continuity sheet establishes a three-level house with the kitchen on the main floor, so the kitchen is one level above the basement, not three. Because the recording is seeded as later evidence, this kind of geography error is high-cost.
findings.rewrite_direction: Correct snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:307 so the recorder's position matches the established house layout and the implied sound path stays plausible. If Nate is on the basement cot, the recorder should be described as on the main-floor kitchen counter, one floor up, or in another specifically named location that fits the geography.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass only if, by the end of the Burnsville exchange in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:251 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:255, a cold reader can answer "What does Martin owe Cliff?" with something more specific than "some business issue" and can tell that the unresolved money matters now.
findings.problem: Cliff's money beat is too euphemistic to cleanly land the required setup that Martin owes him consequential money. "Close out our position on the Burnsville thing" and "try to collect on the other end" sound plausibly Cliff-ish, but the page never cashes out the business static into a legible debt or stalled payout. Later motive depends on that pressure reading as real, not just vaguely transactional.
findings.rewrite_direction: Sharpen snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:251 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:255 so Cliff still sounds like Cliff but the reader can tell, in this scene, that Martin has been sitting on money or an unresolved real-estate exposure Cliff needs resolved. One explicit clause, one sharper ask, or one Nate observation is enough, as long as it converts the euphemism into legible stakes.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass only if, before Nate goes upstairs in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:417, the revised chapter has already established on page that he and Martin customarily settle the last-place bet late at night or that Nate has another concrete, previously introduced reason to look for him. When the body-discovery beat arrives, the reader should be recalling a setup rather than learning it for the first time.
findings.problem: The annual five-dollar side bet appears for the first time at the exact moment the plot needs Nate to go upstairs, so the discovery trigger reads as a convenience insertion rather than as previously established behavior. Because Martin's body discovery is the chapter's major turn, the access mechanism needs setup the reader can recall instead of brand-new information delivered on the threshold.
findings.rewrite_direction: Seed the late-night bet ritual or another specific Nate-to-Martin errand earlier in the chapter, then let snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:415 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:423 pay that setup off. The cleanest places to plant it are in the arrival banter at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:11, the post-draft wind-down at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:175, or the pre-bed exchange at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:399.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if chapter 01 contains at least three exchanges of crude/raunchy humor that go beyond clever wit, Tommy's dialogue profanity density matches his 'very high' profile, and the hot tub scene includes shown dialogue rather than summarized conversation. Fail if the comedy register remains at its current level of polish and moderate profanity.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3-447 — The draft scene, which should be the novel's comedic peak, plays as sharp group banter with moderate profanity. The hot tub scene (lines 315-387) is described as happening 'two bodies past the tub's comfortable capacity' but the dialogue during this scene — which the style bible says should be 'crude, raunchy, profane, sexually explicit' between drunk men in a hot tub — is rendered through narration rather than shown.

5: Nate Griggs heard the house before he saw it. The driveway was a quarter-mile of gravel through pines, and somewhere up ahead Tommy Renaldi was laughing — that big, open-throated laugh that carried across practice fields and parking lots and apparently through woods in rural Minnesota, because Nate could hear it over his engine and the podcast ad for a mattress company he hadn't bothered to pause.
6: 
7: He parked behind Cliff's Audi. The Audi was new, or new-looking — Cliff's cars were always new-looking even when they weren't. Nate grabbed his duffel, his mic bag, and the thirty-rack of Hamm's that was his annual contribution to the weekend. Martin stocked the house with Lagavulin and craft beer, but Nate brought the Hamm's because he'd brought the Hamm's since 2001, and some bits were sacred even when the beer wasn't.

101: He set a ceramic bowl on the table with seven folded slips of paper inside. Martin had cut the paper, written the numbers, folded them identically, because Martin folded things identically. The draft order was random, but the randomness was Martin's — he produced the system, ran it, and never submitted it to review. Nobody had ever accused him of rigging it. Nobody had ever verified it either.
102: 
103: Tommy drew first pick and bellowed. Cliff drew second and said nothing, which meant he was doing math. Doug drew third and said, "Sure, that works." Paul drew fourth and nodded. Nate drew fifth and made a sound that Tommy later described on the podcast clip as "a walrus being told bad news." Joel drew sixth without comment. Seventh was Martin's by default — the last slot, the host's slot, the one that looked like sacrifice — and nobody pointed out that the man who controlled the paper and the bowl had landed there again.

findings.problem: Chapter 1 establishes the group dynamic through clever banter rather than the crude, raunchy register the style bible demands. The hot tub scene — the most natural setting for the requested register — is largely summarized. Tommy's dialogue contains some profanity but nowhere near the 'connective tissue' density his profile specifies. The comedy is funny but clean relative to the style bible's ask.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:100-200 (draft banter) and 315-387 (hot tub scene). Increase Tommy's profanity density to match his 'very high' profile — 'fuck' and 'shit' should appear in most of his casual sentences. Add at least two exchanges of genuinely crude humor — sexual jokes, body humor, the casually cruel ribbing that old male friendships produce. Show the hot tub conversation rather than summarizing it. The goal is not vulgarity for its own sake but the specific texture of men who've been drinking together for 25 years.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 1 contains at most 2 loon references.
findings.evidence: The word 'loon' appears 44 times across 18 chapters (all chapters, 1-4 per chapter). Ch 1 has 4 loon mentions. Not tracked as a motif or setup/payoff in outline/outline.md.
findings.problem: Loon calls at 44 mentions far exceed the 10-mention threshold. Target: keep 8-10 of the strongest instances (e.g., Ch 1 first establishment, Ch 3 'idiot laugh,' Ch 14 discovery, Ch 18 final). Ch 1 has 4; keep 1-2.
findings.rewrite_direction: Across the manuscript, reduce loon mentions from 44 to 8-10. In Ch 1, cut 2-3 of the 4 mentions, retaining one atmospheric establishment.

findings.evidence: 35 em-dash-containing prose lines / 219 total prose lines = 15.9%.

findings.rewrite_direction: Vary sentence openings. Reduce He-openers to below 7%.

findings.acceptance_test: The real estate deal location is named consistently across Chs 1 and 8.
findings.evidence: Ch 1 line 253: 'close out our position on the Burnsville thing for two years'; Ch 8 line 1928: 'a duplex in Roseville that had gone sideways'; Ch 8 line 1980: 'the Roseville property at 1847 Dale St'
findings.problem: The real estate deal between Martin and Cliff is called 'the Burnsville thing' in Ch 1 but identified as being in Roseville in Ch 8. Burnsville and Roseville are different suburbs of Minneapolis, 20+ miles apart.
findings.rewrite_direction: Correct Ch 1's 'the Burnsville thing' to 'the Roseville thing' or adjust Ch 8's location to match. Roseville is established more specifically (1847 Dale St), so correcting Ch 1 is simpler.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: The comedy is the creative risk and the creative achievement. These are men in their late forties who talk the way men in their late forties talk when there are no women present and the drinks have been flowing for hours: crude, raunchy, profane, sexually explicit, casually cruel, and occasionally accidentally beautiful. Do not clean this up. Do not soften the language. Do not make them witty where they would be vulgar. Do not make them eloquent where they would be clumsy. The profanity is not decoration — it is the texture of the friendship. When the second death breaks the comic register, the absence of that profane warmth should feel like a loss, not a relief. The murder, when it arrives, should be rendered with physical specificity — the water, the struggle, the sounds. The grief should be specific. The shame should be specific. The twenty-two-year-old guilt should be embodied, not summarized. If a scene makes the writer uncomfortable, that is a signal to stay in it longer, not to cut away.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Every euphemism for death, sex, bodily function, emotional state, or financial desperation must be replaced with the direct term or a character-specific avoidance that itself reveals something about the character. Cliff saying 'restructure' when he means 'I'm broke' is character work. The narrator describing Cliff as 'facing financial challenges' is sanitization.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the absolute norm. These are informal men in their late forties who have been friends for twenty-five years. Uncontracted forms should appear only for deliberate emphasis, Joel's occasional legal precision, or Mallick's public-persona overcorrection. If two consecutive lines of dialogue contain uncontracted forms and neither character has a stated reason for formality, at least one is wrong.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. After a dialogue-heavy chapter, lead the next with interiority or description. After an interview chapter, give the reader a memory or a quiet observation. After a comic set piece, let the next chapter breathe. The scene counts vary (2-3) and should feel varied — a two-scene chapter after a three-scene chapter creates natural pacing shift. The tonal break at Chapter 14 should feel like a gear change, not a gradual dimming.
prose_style_profile.diction: Muscular, specific, unpretentious. No literary posturing. The prose should sound like intelligent conversation, not workshopped fiction. Prefer concrete nouns over abstract ones. Prefer active constructions. Avoid decorative metaphor — when a metaphor appears, it should be load-bearing. The comedy demands precision: funny is specific, and vague is never funny.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Prestige-literary inflation — do not elevate the comic register into literary solemnity. A man flushing Viagra while a detective knocks on the door is funny. The prose should be funny. Do not describe it as if it were a scene from Dostoevsky.

Comedic exhaustion — do not let every paragraph contain a joke. Comedy needs rhythm, which means it needs non-comic beats to land against. Let some paragraphs be straightforwardly descriptive or emotional.
Interior monologue sprawl — close third is not stream of consciousness. The POV character's interiority should be selective and purposeful, not an open channel of every thought.
Simultaneous narration — do not describe characters 'simultaneously' feeling multiple things. Choose the dominant feeling and let the undertone be implicit.
Forensic procedure porn — this is not a police procedural. Keep investigation details specific but brief. The comedy and character are the vehicle, not the forensics.

Metaphor creep — do not let the same metaphor template appear more than twice in a chapter. If you've used a sports metaphor, a water metaphor, and a fire metaphor, check that none of them are doing the same structural work.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, rotating POV. Each chapter is locked to one character's interiority. No omniscient intrusions. The prose should take on the coloring of the POV character without becoming parody — Nate's chapters move faster and list more, Doug's chapters are quieter and more observational, Mallick's chapters shift between her two registers.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Conversational propulsion, not literary drift. The prose should move like someone telling you this story — not rushed, but never self-admiring. Vary sentence length aggressively: long compound sentences for the comic set pieces, short declarative hits for the turns. The humor lives in rhythm as much as content — the timing of a punchline is a prose-level decision.
sensory_bias: auditory — conversations, lake sounds, house creaks, the specific silence of a house where someone died
proprioceptive — hangovers, physical discomfort, adrenaline, the specific sensation of a body that hasn't slept
olfactory — booze, lake water, morning coffee, the basement must, the casserole
